user.title: Step 1 of 7 — Battery System (Required)

data_description.battery_soc_entity: Must report charge level as a plain percentage, e.g. 74.3. Examples: sensor.battery_soc, sensor.powerwall_charge, sensor.solis_battery_soc. Do NOT pick a power sensor (kW) or voltage sensor (V) — it must be percentage (%).
data_description.battery_capacity_kwh: Usable capacity in kWh from your battery datasheet. Common sizes: Tesla Powerwall 2 = 13.5 kWh · BYD HVS 10 = 10.2 kWh · Sonnen eco 8 = 8 kWh · GivEnergy 9.5 = 9.5 kWh. Use the *usable* figure, not the total (they differ by ~5–10%).
data_description.min_soc_floor_percent: The battery will never discharge below this level. Recommended: 10–20% to protect battery health and keep an emergency reserve. Your inverter may also have its own hard minimum — this setting should be at or above that.

export_window.title: Step 2 of 7 — Export Bonus Window & Rates (Required)
export_window.description: Enter your electricity retailer's **peak feed-in tariff (FiT) window** and rates. Enter rates as a decimal — e.g. enter **0.45** for 45 cents/kWh or £0.45/kWh. Check your electricity bill or retailer's website.

data.export_bonus_rate: Export bonus rate (per kWh, during bonus window)
data.standard_export_rate: Standard export rate (per kWh, outside bonus window)
data.standard_import_rate: Standard import rate (per kWh, what you pay to buy from grid)

data_description.export_bonus_start: When your retailer's high feed-in rate starts. Examples: 16:00 (Amber evening peak) · 17:00 (Intelligent Octopus dispatch) · 18:00 (many SEG tariffs).
data_description.export_bonus_end: When the bonus window ends. Examples: 20:00 · 21:00. Set start = end (e.g. both 00:00) if you have no bonus window — leave the rate at 0.
data_description.export_bonus_rate: Rate you earn per kWh exported during the bonus window. Examples: 0.45 (45p/kWh UK peak FiT) · 1.20 (120c/kWh Amber spot peak) · 0.60 (60c AUS peak). Check your retailer's FiT schedule.
data_description.standard_export_rate: Rate you earn per kWh for exports outside the bonus window. Examples: 0.05 (5p/kWh standard SEG) · 0.08 · 0.12. Often much lower than the bonus rate.
data_description.standard_import_rate: What you normally pay per kWh to buy from the grid. Examples: 0.28 (28p/kWh UK average) · 0.30 (30c US average) · 0.35 (35c AUS average). Use your off-peak rate if you have a time-of-use tariff.

solar_forecast.title: Step 3 of 7 — Solar Forecast (Required)

data_description.solar_forecast_entity: Pick the sensor that provides *future* solar predictions, not just today's total. Forecast.Solar: sensor.energy_production_today (has 'wh_period' or 'watts' attributes). Solcast: sensor.solcast_pv_forecast_today (has 'detailedForecast' attribute). Generic: any sensor whose state is today's expected kWh total. The format is detected automatically — you just need to pick the right entity.

optional_tariffs.title: Step 4 of 7 — Additional Tariff Periods (Optional)
optional_tariffs.description:
Configure time-of-use rate windows if your tariff has them. **Leave all fields blank** if you have a simple flat-rate tariff.

Common examples: Octopus Go / Intelligent (cheap overnight 23:30–05:30) · Economy 7 (01:00–08:00) · Amber off-peak. Configuring these helps the optimizer decide the best times to charge from the grid.

data.peak_import_start: Peak import window — start (most expensive hours)
data.peak_import_rate: Peak import rate (per kWh, during peak hours)
data.grid_charging_enabled: Allow grid charging during the cheap import window

data_description.free_import_start: Start of your cheap or free import window. Examples: 23:30 (Octopus Go) · 01:00 (Economy 7) · 11:00 (midday solar soak). Leave blank if you have no cheap window.
data_description.free_import_end: End of the cheap window. Examples: 05:30 (Octopus Go) · 08:00 (Economy 7) · 14:00.
data_description.peak_import_start: Start of your most expensive import period. Examples: 16:00 · 17:00. Leave blank if your tariff has no peak rate.
data_description.peak_import_end: End of the peak import period. Examples: 20:00 · 21:00.
data_description.peak_import_rate: What you pay per kWh during peak hours. Examples: 0.55 (55p/kWh UK peak) · 0.65 · 0.80. Should be higher than your standard import rate.
data_description.grid_charging_enabled: Let the optimizer charge the battery from the grid during the cheap window above. Only enable if grid charging is cost-effective on your tariff (i.e. the cheap rate + battery cycle cost is less than the peak export rate you'll earn).

optional_consumption.title: Step 5 of 7 — Home Consumption (Optional)
optional_consumption.description:
Help the optimizer predict how much energy your home will use. It learns your patterns from history (weekdays vs weekends, morning vs evening).

Tip — kW vs kWh: The **sensor** must be cumulative **kWh** (energy, goes up over time). The **baseline** below is in **kW** (power, your average draw right now).

data.consumption_entity: Home consumption sensor (cumulative kWh — must increase over time)
data.consumption_baseline_kw: Fallback baseline — average home power draw (kW)
data.consumption_lookback_days: Days of history to learn from

data_description.consumption_entity: A sensor that measures total grid energy consumed — it must increase over time as you use electricity. Examples: sensor.grid_consumption_kwh · sensor.home_energy_meter · sensor.electricity_meter_total. DO NOT pick a power sensor in kW or W — those show instantaneous draw, not cumulative energy. Check the sensor's unit: it must be kWh.
data_description.consumption_baseline_kw: Your home's average power draw in kilowatts (kW). This is used before patterns are learned and as a sanity-check fallback. Typical values: 0.4–0.7 kW (small flat, efficient home) · 0.8–1.2 kW (average house) · 1.5–2.5 kW (large home or with EV/heat pump). Remember: 1 kW for 1 hour = 1 kWh.
data_description.consumption_profile_granularity: How the optimizer groups your usage history. 'Weekday/Weekend' is recommended — most homes use more energy on weekdays (commute patterns, heating schedules). 'Full 7-day' gives the most detail but needs more data (90+ days).
data_description.consumption_lookback_days: How many days of recorder history to train on. 30 days captures recent patterns. 90 days smooths out short-term anomalies. Use 180+ days to capture seasonal variation (summer vs winter usage).

optional_weather.title: Step 6 of 7 — Weather Integration (Optional)
optional_weather.description: Connect a weather entity to improve solar forecast confidence. On overcast days the optimizer will trust the solar forecast less and keep more charge in reserve. It also adjusts predicted home load based on temperature (more heating in winter, more cooling in summer).

data_description.weather_entity: Any Home Assistant weather integration works. Examples: weather.home (Met.no, default in most HA installs) · weather.openweathermap · weather.pirateweather · weather.accuweather. If you have multiple, pick the one with the best local accuracy for your site.

optional_battery_rates.title: Step 7 of 7 — Inverter Power Limits (Optional)
optional_battery_rates.description:
Set how fast your inverter can charge, discharge, and export. These are **power limits in kW** (not energy in kWh).

Tip: kW = rate of flow (like water flow rate). kWh = total energy (like litres). A 5 kW inverter charging for 2 hours stores 10 kWh.

The defaults (5 kW) suit most 5 kW hybrid inverters. Check your inverter datasheet if unsure.

data.max_charge_rate_kw: Max charge rate (kW) — how fast the inverter charges the battery
data.max_discharge_rate_kw: Max discharge rate (kW) — how fast the inverter can discharge
data.max_export_limit_kw: Max export to grid (kW) — network or retailer export cap
data.max_export_limit_entity: Dynamic export limit sensor (kW) — overrides the static cap above

data_description.max_charge_rate_kw: Maximum charge power in kW from your inverter datasheet. Examples: 3.6 kW (Solax X1) · 5.0 kW (GivEnergy 5kW, Solis 5G, Sungrow SH5) · 6.0 kW (GivEnergy 6kW) · 7.5 kW (Tesla Powerwall 2). This is a POWER value (kW) — not the battery size (kWh).
data_description.max_discharge_rate_kw: Maximum discharge power in kW. Usually the same as the charge rate for hybrid inverters. Examples: 5.0 · 6.0 · 7.5. Some batteries have asymmetric charge/discharge limits — check your datasheet.
data_description.max_export_limit_kw: Maximum power your system can send to the grid in kW. This is usually set by your network operator or DNO, not your inverter. Examples: 3.68 kW (common UK G98 limit for single-phase) · 5.0 · 10.0 (if approved for higher export). Check your grid connection agreement.
data_description.max_export_limit_entity: Optional: a sensor that provides a real-time export limit in kW. Useful for dynamic export control (DRED, Agile pricing-based curtailment, or DNO demand response). Example: sensor.export_cap_kw. Leave blank to use the fixed limit above.

error.entity_not_found: Entity not found — make sure it exists and is available in Home Assistant.

data.aggressiveness: Aggressiveness (0 = hold charge, 1 = maximise export revenue)

data_description.aggressiveness: Trade-off between keeping charge for home use vs exporting for income. 0.0 = very conservative (holds charge). 0.3–0.5 = balanced (good default). 1.0 = aggressive exporter. Start at 0.3 and adjust based on your results.
data_description.slot_granularity_minutes: Time resolution of the schedule. 30 minutes is recommended — matches most time-of-use tariff boundaries and solar forecast granularity. Use 15 minutes only if your tariff has 15-minute intervals.
data_description.lookahead_hours: How far ahead the optimizer plans. 24 hours covers one day. 48 hours is useful with multi-day solar forecasts (Solcast provides 7 days). Longer lookahead increases solve time.
data_description.recalculation_interval_minutes: How often the full schedule is recalculated from scratch. 30 minutes is a good balance. The schedule also recalculates automatically when the solar forecast changes.
data_description.bridge_to_fallback_time: If the solar forecast is unavailable, the optimizer ensures there's enough charge to reach this time (typically the next sunrise). Example: 06:00. This prevents running flat overnight when forecast data is missing.

data.grid_charging_enabled: Allow grid charging during cheap import window

data.battery_soc_entity: Battery SOC sensor (% — charge level, not power)
data.solar_forecast_entity: Solar forecast sensor (with hourly forecast in attributes)
data.consumption_entity: Home consumption sensor (cumulative kWh — not power in kW)

data_description.battery_soc_entity: Must report 0–100 (percentage). NOT a power sensor (kW) or voltage (V).
data_description.solar_forecast_entity: Forecast.Solar or Solcast sensor with per-hour forecast data in attributes. Format auto-detected.
data_description.consumption_entity: Cumulative energy sensor in kWh that increases over time. NOT instantaneous power in kW or W.
data_description.weather_entity: Any HA weather entity — provides cloud cover and temperature for forecast adjustment.
data_description.max_export_limit_entity: Optional real-time export cap sensor in kW. Overrides the static export limit.

data.fallback_mode: Fallback behaviour when optimizer cannot run

data_description.fallback_mode: What to do when the optimizer fails (missing forecast, solver error). 'Conservative hold' — stop charging/discharging and hold the battery at its current level. 'Last known good' — replay the most recent successful schedule. 'Error state' — mark as unavailable.
data_description.solver_timeout_seconds: Maximum seconds allowed for the LP solver per cycle. Increase to 60–120 on slow hardware (e.g. Raspberry Pi 3) if you see solver timeout errors in the logs.
data_description.data_retention_days: How many days of planned-vs-actual tracking data to keep. 90 days is a good default. Increase to 365 for year-round seasonal analysis.
data_description.min_soc_floor_percent: Hard lower limit — the battery will never be scheduled below this SOC. Acts as a safety reserve and battery health protection. Should be at or above your inverter's own minimum.
